author: @your_utube
text:
A simple, classical device of high utility, made with homebred diy knowhow from a generation who still knew how to do things with their own hands. And to think it wasn't even made in China!
Thanks Robert for highlighting how being practical and skillful can still be commonplace.

author: @decyrano
text: A small allthread and nut in those pre existing holes would stabilize that overly large compass for a more accurate circle. Friction at the apex is not enough to guarantee no enlargement of the circle. That is also how my commercially made compass is constructed, though only about half the overall length of yours.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did think about it mate and if you made this in metal I would guess it would be essential - but It was firm enough for my needs and I try to keep things simple if I can - however I can do an update on doing what you suggest from found materials if folks like

author: @tinkmarshino
text: Brilliant! I have some very old Philippine mahogany that was part of my grandfathers wood stock he had before he died.. I have had that and other exotic woods he had in my shop for 50 years now.. It is time to use the left over mahogany for one last project.. Thanks for the tip on a great christmas gift...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are so lucky mate! I am willing to bet I would be very envious of your Woodstock if I saw it lol

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Absolutely brilliant. Looks beautiful also, functionality and beauty don't often come in the same package (especially true for people). 
You could get your knife and cut thin grooves on both sides for the top adjustment inners to get a non-slip positional lock.
I love making my own tools also, I have made a couple of hardwood hand plane's, even the blade was made from scrap steel which I hardened.
Yet another great video Rob, keep them coming, I enjoy sitting down with my morning coffee and watching your videos. My morning ritual 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - cheers you must do some videos I would love to see - cheers

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering trust me you wouldn't, I don't have a decent camera or the room to do it. My bench is small and untidy and as a sole parent my 10 year old son would be interrupting constantly. Also I would be fumbling through because I have most fingers amputated and I wouldn't have any content anywhere near the quality of yours. I have plenty of ideas but projects take ages to complete. I am teaching myself Electronics and learn from others on YouTube.
Damn I am full of excuses ... 🙃

author: @philipvernejules9926
text:
........To actually make a compass . I never considered it . I made do with using an engineering scribe with points at both ends and scratching the circle outline into the wood . Then for bigger circles I'd use big cans or plates or buckets or lids or bowls and even an idea suggested once in your videos by someone  of using a length of scrap polystyrene  and plunging a pencil and nail through at desired points .
 .In my shopping , a compass has never been on my list of things but I ain't ever noticed them while perusing either. If I made one ma

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think the nail in a scrap is just fine mate - but after a bit I just wanted a big compass and like you I just never thought of buying one but then I was looking at the mahogany scraps and it suddenly struck me lol

author: @RoadRageLive
text: A compass, I thought you meant a compass.... You know, north south...  I love the way you are using the resources that you have or the stuff that others are getting rid of.  I think your compass is really awesome. It beats using string and lots of luck.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: It goes to show that with a bit of imagination and saving decent scrap material you can make anything to get the job done. Also it doesn't need to look like it was just thrown together.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - yeah titles can be a pain to get right - I am glad you like it mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It was done with a hand saw, drill, carpet knife and sand paper mate - I also like to try and do stuff with minimal tooling

author: @sailingoctopus1
text: More properly, it should be called a pair of compasses.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Why, there is only one of them. One leg on its own has no pivot point.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we called it a set or a dividing compass I think - but that's going back a few years lol

replies.author: @sailingoctopus1
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, at school (50 years ago in the UK) we had 'dividers' that were symmetrical, with two points, but no pencil, and are used for bisecting not for drawing circles or arcs. We were taught that if they had a pencil and used for drawing circles and arcs they are called a pair of compasses, although I think this term is falling out of use.
